_comment: Hand-curated 'gold' hint sets used two ways: (1) a few are injected as before/after few-shot into hint_prompt.txt / hint_judge_prompt.txt to calibrate the local model, and (2) the whole set is the calibration bench for scripts/improve_hints.py --calibrate: the Qwen judge should label every `leak` example as 'reveals' and every `gold` hint as 'ok'. See docs/hint-generation.md. Each entry: slug, gist (approximate problem summary, NOT the live statement), optional leak (a real too-revealing hint pulled from the old bank) + why, and gold (the target 3-tier hint set: nudge / technique / insight-without-transition).

exemplars.gist: Given coin denominations (each reusable) and a target amount, return the fewest coins that sum to it, or -1 if impossible.
exemplars.leak: For each amount, iterate through all coins and find the minimum previous result plus one.
exemplars.why_leak: States the exact recurrence (min over coins of dp[a-c] + 1); the solver has nothing left to derive or implement.
gold: Since coins are reusable, the best way to form an amount is closely tied to the best way to form smaller amounts.
This is an optimal-substructure problem: consider solving it for every amount from 0 up to the target, not just the target.
Keep an array indexed by amount holding the fewest coins to form it; amount 0 needs none, and some amounts stay impossible to form at all.

exemplars.gist: Decide whether a string of brackets ()[]{} is properly opened and closed.
exemplars.leak: Iterate through the string. Push open brackets onto the stack. For closing brackets, pop and verify the match. The string is valid if the stack is empty at the end.
exemplars.why_leak: Spells out the complete algorithm end to end, including the terminal condition; it is prose pseudocode, not a nudge.
gold: The most recently opened bracket must be the first one to close — order matters in a specific way.
That last-in, first-out behavior is exactly what a stack captures.
As you scan, each closing bracket must pair with the most recent still-open bracket — so what should happen the moment one doesn't match?

exemplars.gist: Given bar heights forming an elevation map, compute how much rainwater is trapped.
exemplars.leak: Calculate prefix maximums from the left and suffix maximums from the right, then sum the trapped water at each index.
exemplars.why_leak: Hands over the entire solution structure; it reveals both the key quantity and the exact way to compute it, leaving nothing to discover.
gold: Focus on a single position: what determines how much water can sit on top of it?
Water above an index is capped by the tallest bars to its left and to its right.
It is capped by the shorter of those two flanking maxima — so the only real challenge left is knowing both of them for every bar without rescanning each time.

exemplars.gist: Return the indices of the two numbers in an array that add up to a target.
gold: For each number there is exactly one other value that would complete the pair — what is it?
A hash map lets you check in one step whether a value you need has already appeared.
By the time you reach a number, any valid partner is one you have already passed — so what is the single thing worth checking before you move on from it?

exemplars.gist: Minimum number of insert/delete/replace operations to turn one string into another.
exemplars.leak: Consider how the problem breaks down into smaller subproblems involving prefixes of both strings.
exemplars.why_leak: Too vague, not too revealing — this could be pasted onto almost any string DP; it names no concrete handle for THIS problem.
gold: Think about transforming just prefixes of the two strings, growing them one character at a time.
This is a classic two-dimensional dynamic program indexed by the two prefix lengths.
The answer for a pair of prefixes turns on whether their last characters match and on three nearby smaller subproblems — one per edit operation.

exemplars.gist: Count connected groups of land cells ('1') in a 2D grid.
gold: Each island is a maximal blob of connected land — how do you make sure you count a blob only once?
A flood fill (DFS or BFS) starting at one land cell can reach and mark that cell's whole island.
If you mark an island completely the first time you touch it, any still-unmarked land you meet later must belong to a brand-new one — so the answer is just how many fresh starts you make.

exemplars.gist: Given prerequisite pairs, decide whether all courses can be finished (no cyclic dependency).
exemplars.leak: Use topological sorting via Kahn's algorithm or DFS. Track visited nodes and in-degrees to detect back-edges.
exemplars.why_leak: Names the exact algorithm(s) and all of the bookkeeping; it is an implementation checklist rather than an insight.
gold: Model courses and prerequisites as a directed graph, then ask what structure would make finishing impossible.
You can finish everything exactly when the dependency graph contains no cycle.
So the task reduces to detecting a cycle in a directed graph — think about ordering nodes so every prerequisite comes first, and what it means when no such order exists.

exemplars.gist: Count the distinct ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time.
gold: Think about the very last step you take to reach the top — how many options are there for it?
The number of ways to reach a stair is fixed once you know the answers for the few stairs just below it — a bottom-up one-dimensional count.
That yields a Fibonacci-like relation you can roll up from the bottom with only a couple of running values.

exemplars.gist: Decide whether a string can be segmented into a sequence of dictionary words.
gold: Try to decide segmentability of the whole string in terms of its prefixes.
Frame it as reachability over positions: which cut points in the string can be reached as the end of a valid segmentation? Build that up left to right.
The crux at each position is whether some dictionary word ends there beginning from an already-reachable point — that overlap is what makes solving each prefix once pay off.

exemplars.gist: Find the maximum path sum between any two nodes in a binary tree (a path need not pass through the root).
gold: A path can bend at a node and dip into both children, but what can that node hand back up to its parent?
Use a post-order traversal in which each node reports the best single downward branch it can offer.
Keep a separate global best that, at each node, may join the node with its two best downward branches — even though only one branch can be passed upward.

exemplars.gist: Return the longest palindromic substring (smallest start index breaks ties).
exemplars.leak: How can we reuse a previously computed palindrome to compute a larger palindrome?
exemplars.why_leak: Too thin — a lone rhetorical question that quietly forces the O(n^2) DP framing and gives no foothold for actually starting.
gold: Every palindrome is symmetric around a center — how many possible centers does the string have?
Instead of checking all substrings, grow outward from each candidate center while the two sides keep matching.
Remember centers come in two flavors — on a single character and between two characters — so both must be tried.

exemplars.gist: Burst balloons for coins nums[left]*nums[i]*nums[right] (adjacent surviving balloons); maximize total coins.
exemplars.leak: To compute dp[i][j], iterate through every possible last balloon k in the range. The coins earned are nums[i]*nums[k]*nums[j] plus the optimal results from the left and right sub-ranges.
exemplars.why_leak: The full interval-DP transition, including the exact product and the two recursive terms — it is the solution transcribed.
gold: Bursting order is confusing because neighbors keep changing — try reasoning about which balloon in a range is burst last, not first.
For a range of balloons, the one popped last has clean, fixed neighbors: the balloons just outside that range.
That turns it into an interval dynamic program over subranges, where you pick the last balloon to burst and combine the two independent sides.

exemplars.gist: Given an array, decide whether there exist indices i<j<k with nums[i] < nums[k] < nums[j] (a 132 pattern).
exemplars.leak: Use a monotonic stack to maintain candidates for nums[j]. When a smaller value arrives, it acts as nums[k]; pop larger values from the stack to find the best nums[j] that satisfies the condition with the current minimum.
exemplars.why_leak: Names the structure AND narrates the pop/compare loop that operates it — a student could transcribe the loop body directly, even with no literal code and no 'first/then' wording. This is the mechanism-narration failure mode.
gold: Fix each element as the middle, largest value of the pattern — what must exist before it, and what must exist after it?
A stack of candidate peaks scanned in one pass can track this without rechecking every triple.
You need the smallest value seen before the peak and, after it, some value landing strictly between that minimum and the peak — think about what is cheapest to remember as you scan.

exemplars.gist: Two vertical lines and the x-axis form a container; choose two lines to maximize the water area.
gold: The area a pair holds is set by the shorter of the two lines and how far apart they stand.
Start from the widest possible pair and ask which side it could ever help to move inward.
Because the shorter line caps the height, moving the taller boundary inward sacrifices width for no possible gain — the entire upside sits on the shorter side.

exemplars.gist: Find the minimum eating speed so Koko finishes all banana piles within h hours.
gold: The faster Koko eats, the fewer hours she needs — feasibility only ever improves with speed.
That monotonicity means you can search over candidate speeds instead of trying them one by one.
Binary-search the speed, and for each candidate check whether the hours it would take fit within the limit.

exemplars.gist: Return the length of the longest substring containing no repeated character.
gold: A run of distinct characters can only extend so far before a repeat forces you to abandon its left end.
Maintain a window and push its right edge forward, pulling the left edge in whenever a character repeats.
Remember which characters are currently inside the window so you know exactly how far to pull the left edge.

exemplars.gist: Each value is a maximum jump length from that index; decide whether the last index is reachable.
gold: Rather than exploring every possible sequence of jumps, track a single quantity as you scan left to right.
Keep the farthest index reachable so far, updating it at every position you can still get to.
The only way to fail is to stand on a spot your running reach has not already covered — so everything hinges on that reach never falling behind your position.
